expanded_description: A comprehensive examination of immune disorders—conditions arising from dysregulation of the immune system—detailing the four types of hypersensitivity reactions (Type I—immediate IgE-mediated, Type II—antibody-mediated cytotoxic, Type III—immune complex-mediated, Type IV—delayed T cell-mediated), the spectrum of autoimmune diseases (organ-specific and systemic) resulting from loss of self-tolerance, and immunodeficiencies (primary—genetic defects; secondary—acquired) leading to increased susceptibility to infections. Emphasis is placed on the underlying immunological mechanisms, clinical presentations, diagnostic approaches, and therapeutic strategies (immunosuppression, replacement therapy, targeted biologics).

core_concept.principle: Immune disorders arise from imbalance in immune function: hypersensitivity and autoimmune diseases result from excessive or misdirected immune responses (overactivity against foreign antigens or self-antigens), while immunodeficiencies result from insufficient immune responses (increased susceptibility to infections)—with the clinical manifestations determined by the underlying mechanism, affected cell types, and target tissues.
core_concept.explanation:
The immune system is a finely balanced network that protects against pathogens while maintaining tolerance to self. Immune disorders occur when this balance is disrupted, leading to either excessive immune activity (hypersensitivity, autoimmunity) or insufficient activity (immunodeficiency).

Hypersensitivity reactions (Gell and Coombs classification) are exaggerated or inappropriate immune responses to foreign antigens (allergens) or self-antigens, categorized by mechanism:

Type I (Immediate, IgE-mediated): Antigen cross-links IgE on mast cells/basophils → degranulation → histamine, leukotrienes, prostaglandins → vasodilation, bronchoconstriction, mucus secretion. Clinical: anaphylaxis, allergic rhinitis, asthma, urticaria.

Type II (Antibody-mediated cytotoxic): IgG/IgM antibodies bind cell surface antigens → complement activation (cell lysis), opsonization (phagocytosis), or ADCC. Clinical: autoimmune hemolytic anemia, immune thrombocytopenia, Goodpasture syndrome, Graves' disease (stimulatory antibody).

Type III (Immune complex-mediated): Antigen-antibody complexes deposit in tissues → complement activation → neutrophil recruitment → tissue damage. Clinical: serum sickness, systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE), post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis.

Type IV (Delayed-type, T cell-mediated): Antigen-sensitized T cells (CD4+ Th1/Th17 or CD8+ CTL) mediate inflammation 24-72 hours after exposure. Clinical: contact dermatitis (poison ivy), tuberculin skin test, type 1 diabetes, multiple sclerosis.

Autoimmune diseases result from breakdown of self-tolerance, leading to immune attack on self-antigens. Mechanisms include: failure of central tolerance (thymic negative selection), failure of peripheral tolerance (Treg dysfunction), molecular mimicry (cross-reactive antigens), epitope spreading, and genetic susceptibility (HLA associations). Autoimmune diseases are classified as organ-specific (Hashimoto's thyroiditis, type 1 diabetes, multiple sclerosis) or systemic (SLE, rheumatoid arthritis, systemic sclerosis).

Immunodeficiencies result from defects in immune system components, leading to increased susceptibility to infections. Primary immunodeficiencies (PIDs) are genetic disorders presenting in infancy or childhood (though some present in adults). Categories: B cell deficiencies (X-linked agammaglobulinemia—recurrent encapsulated bacteria), T cell deficiencies (DiGeorge syndrome—viral/fungal infections), combined deficiencies (SCID—severe infections), phagocyte deficiencies (chronic granulomatous disease—catalase-positive bacteria/fungi), complement deficiencies (encapsulated bacteria, Neisseria), and innate immune deficiencies (TLR3 deficiency—HSV encephalitis). Secondary immunodeficiencies are acquired: HIV/AIDS (CD4+ T cell depletion), chemotherapy/radiation, immunosuppressive drugs, malnutrition, splenectomy.

Understanding immune disorders is essential for clinical practice: diagnosing and managing allergic diseases, autoimmune conditions, and immunodeficiencies; selecting appropriate immunosuppressive therapies; recognizing infection patterns that suggest immunodeficiency; and implementing preventive measures (vaccination, antimicrobial prophylaxis).

image.alt: Diagram showing the spectrum of immune disorders: Hypersensitivity (Types I-IV mechanisms), Autoimmune diseases (organ-specific and systemic), Immunodeficiencies (primary and secondary). Clinical examples and mechanisms illustrated.
image.caption: Immune Disorders: Hypersensitivity (excessive responses to foreign or self-antigens), Autoimmunity (loss of self-tolerance), Immunodeficiency (defective immune function). Each category has distinct mechanisms, clinical presentations, and treatment approaches.

sections.section_title: Introduction: The Spectrum of Immune Dysregulation
sections.introduction:
The immune system is a double-edged sword: while essential for host defense, its dysregulation leads to a broad spectrum of disorders. On one end of the spectrum, excessive or misdirected immune responses cause hypersensitivity reactions (allergy) and autoimmune diseases (attack on self). On the other end, deficient immune responses cause immunodeficiencies, rendering individuals vulnerable to infections. Between these extremes lie conditions where immune dysregulation contributes to chronic inflammation, cancer, and transplant rejection.

Hypersensitivity reactions (Gell and Coombs classification) categorize immune-mediated tissue injury by mechanism, providing a framework for understanding allergic diseases, autoimmune cytopenias, immune complex diseases, and T cell-mediated inflammatory conditions. Autoimmune diseases affect 5-10% of the population, with increasing prevalence; they arise from failure of self-tolerance mechanisms and are often associated with specific HLA alleles. Immunodeficiencies, while individually rare, collectively affect 1 in 1,200-2,000 births for primary immunodeficiencies; secondary immunodeficiencies (particularly HIV/AIDS) have global impact.

Clinical recognition of immune disorders depends on pattern recognition: anaphylaxis and angioedema suggest Type I hypersensitivity; autoimmune hemolytic anemia suggests Type II; systemic lupus erythematosus suggests Type III; contact dermatitis suggests Type IV. Recurrent infections with unusual organisms suggest immunodeficiency: encapsulated bacteria (B cell defects), viral/fungal (T cell defects), catalase-positive bacteria/fungi (phagocyte defects), Neisseria (complement defects).

Therapeutic strategies target the underlying immune mechanism: immunosuppression for autoimmunity (corticosteroids, disease-modifying antirheumatic drugs—DMARDs, biologics), allergen avoidance and symptomatic treatment for allergy, antimicrobial prophylaxis and replacement therapy for immunodeficiency, and hematopoietic stem cell transplantation (HSCT) for severe primary immunodeficiencies.

Understanding immune disorders is foundational for internal medicine, allergy/immunology, rheumatology, infectious disease, and pediatrics.

introduction_image.alt: Spectrum of immune function: Hypersensitivity (overactive), Autoimmunity (mis-directed), Normal immunity, Immunodeficiency (underactive). Clinical examples: anaphylaxis, SLE, recurrent infections.
introduction_image.caption: Spectrum of Immune Dysregulation: Excessive immune activity leads to hypersensitivity and autoimmunity; deficient activity leads to immunodeficiency. Understanding the spectrum guides diagnosis and treatment.

key_concept.definition: Hypersensitivity reactions are classified by mechanism (Types I-IV); autoimmune diseases result from loss of self-tolerance; immunodeficiencies result from genetic or acquired defects in immune components—each with distinct clinical presentations, diagnostic approaches, and treatments.
key_concept.context:
The Gell and Coombs classification of hypersensitivity provides a mechanistic framework for understanding immune-mediated tissue injury. Types I-III are antibody-mediated; Type IV is cell-mediated. Autoimmune diseases may involve multiple hypersensitivity mechanisms (SLE: Type II, III, and IV). Primary immunodeficiencies present with characteristic infection patterns based on the affected immune component; diagnosis often requires specialized testing (flow cytometry, genetic analysis).

Clinical relevance: Recognizing the mechanism of immune dysfunction guides treatment: Type I—antihistamines, epinephrine, mast cell stabilizers, biologics (omalizumab); Type II/III—corticosteroids, immunosuppressants, plasmapheresis, rituximab; Type IV—topical corticosteroids, calcineurin inhibitors, biologics (anti-TNF, anti-IL-17). Autoimmune diseases are treated with immunosuppressive and immunomodulatory therapies. Immunodeficiencies are managed with antimicrobial prophylaxis, immunoglobulin replacement, and HSCT for severe cases.

image.alt: Gell and Coombs classification table: Type I (IgE, mast cells, anaphylaxis); Type II (IgG/IgM, complement, cytotoxic); Type III (immune complexes, complement, serum sickness); Type IV (T cells, delayed, contact dermatitis).
image.caption: Hypersensitivity Classification (Gell and Coombs): Types I-IV differ in mechanism (antibody vs. cell-mediated), time course, and clinical manifestations. Understanding the mechanism guides therapeutic targeting.

sections.key_concept: Hypersensitivity reactions are classified into four types based on immune mechanism; autoimmune diseases arise from breakdown of self-tolerance; immunodeficiencies result from defects in immune development or function.

classifications.type: Hypersensitivity: Type I (IgE-Mediated) and Type IV (T Cell-Mediated)
classifications.value: Best for understanding allergic diseases (Type I) and contact dermatitis/tuberculin reactions (Type IV), representing the extremes of antibody-mediated and cell-mediated hypersensitivity.
characteristics: Type I (Immediate, IgE-Mediated): Mechanism—antigen cross-links IgE on sensitized mast cells/basophils → degranulation (preformed mediators: histamine, tryptase, heparin) → synthesis of lipid mediators (leukotrienes, prostaglandins, PAF) → cytokine production (IL-4, IL-5, IL-13). Clinical phases: Immediate (minutes)—vasodilation, edema, bronchospasm; Late-phase (4-6 hours)—eosinophil infiltration, mucosal edema. Clinical syndromes: Anaphylaxis (systemic), urticaria/angioedema (cutaneous), allergic rhinitis (nasal), asthma (bronchial), food allergy (gastrointestinal).

Type IV (Delayed-Type, T Cell-Mediated): Mechanism—antigen-sensitized T cells mediate inflammation 24-72 hours after exposure. Subtypes: Type IVa (Th1, IFN-γ, macrophage activation)—tuberculin reaction, contact dermatitis; Type IVb (Th2, IL-4, IL-5, eosinophil inflammation)—chronic asthma, allergic rhinitis late phase; Type IVc (CD8+ CTL, perforin/granzyme)—contact dermatitis, viral exanthems; Type IVd (γδ T cells, IL-17, neutrophil recruitment)—some drug reactions (Stevens-Johnson syndrome). Clinical: contact dermatitis (poison ivy, nickel), tuberculin skin test, granulomatous diseases (sarcoidosis, Crohn's), drug hypersensitivity (DRESS, SJS/TEN).
classifications.behavior:
Type I hypersensitivity is the most common form of immune-mediated disease, affecting up to 30% of the population. Atopy—the genetic predisposition to produce IgE against common environmental allergens—underlies allergic rhinitis, asthma, and atopic dermatitis. Sensitization occurs when allergens (pollen, dust mites, foods) are presented by dendritic cells to Th2 cells, which produce IL-4, IL-5, and IL-13, promoting IgE class switching in B cells. IgE binds FcεRI on mast cells and basophils, sensitizing these cells for subsequent responses.

Type IV hypersensitivity is mediated by T cells rather than antibodies. In contact dermatitis, haptens (small molecules like urushiol from poison ivy, nickel) penetrate skin, bind self-proteins, are processed by Langerhans cells, and presented to CD4+ and CD8+ T cells. Upon re-exposure, effector T cells migrate to skin and release cytokines (IFN-γ, TNF, IL-17), recruiting macrophages and neutrophils, causing inflammation.

Clinical correlations: Anaphylaxis is a medical emergency requiring immediate epinephrine; biphasic reactions may occur hours later. Asthma is a chronic inflammatory disease with airway hyperresponsiveness; treatment includes inhaled corticosteroids, bronchodilators, and biologics (anti-IgE, anti-IL-5, anti-IL-4Rα). Contact dermatitis diagnosis is confirmed by patch testing; treatment involves allergen avoidance and topical corticosteroids.

examples.explanation:
Anaphylaxis is a severe, life-threatening Type I hypersensitivity reaction: (1) Mechanism: Allergen (food, medication, insect venom) cross-links IgE on mast cells and basophils → massive degranulation and mediator release. (2) Mediators: Histamine—vasodilation, increased permeability, bronchoconstriction, pruritus; Leukotrienes—prolonged bronchoconstriction; Prostaglandins—vasodilation, pain; Platelet-activating factor (PAF)—hypotension, shock; Tryptase—marker of mast cell activation. (3) Clinical: Cutaneous (urticaria, angioedema, flushing); Respiratory (wheezing, stridor, dyspnea, hypoxia); Cardiovascular (hypotension, tachycardia, shock); Gastrointestinal (nausea, vomiting, diarrhea). (4) Diagnosis: Clinical; serum tryptase elevated post-event (mast cell degranulation). (5) Treatment: Epinephrine IM (first-line, α-1 vasoconstriction, β-2 bronchodilation), antihistamines (H1 and H2 blockers), corticosteroids (prevent biphasic reactions), IV fluids. (6) Prevention: Allergen avoidance, allergen immunotherapy (desensitization) for venom and some food allergies, epinephrine auto-injector (EpiPen).

Significance: Anaphylaxis illustrates the systemic effects of mast cell degranulation and the critical role of epinephrine as first-line treatment. Understanding the pathophysiology guides acute management and prevention.

image.alt: Anaphylaxis mechanism: allergen cross-links IgE on mast cell → degranulation → histamine, leukotrienes, PAF → vasodilation, bronchoconstriction, shock. Clinical features: urticaria, wheezing, hypotension. Treatment: epinephrine IM.
image.caption: Anaphylaxis: Type I hypersensitivity emergency. IgE-mediated mast cell degranulation releases mediators causing vasodilation, bronchoconstriction, and shock. Epinephrine is first-line treatment.

examples.explanation:
Contact dermatitis is a classic Type IV hypersensitivity reaction: (1) Sensitization phase: Hapten (e.g., urushiol from poison ivy, nickel) penetrates skin, binds self-proteins, processed by Langerhans cells, presented to naive T cells in lymph nodes → clonal expansion of hapten-specific T cells (CD4+ Th1, CD8+ CTL). (2) Elicitation phase (24-72 hours after re-exposure): Hapten-specific T cells migrate to skin; CD4+ T cells release IFN-γ, TNF, IL-17, activating macrophages; CD8+ CTLs kill keratinocytes. (3) Clinical: Erythema, edema, vesicles, pruritus at contact site; linear lesions (poison ivy), localized to area of exposure. (4) Diagnosis: Patch testing—allergens applied to skin under occlusion for 48 hours; reading at 48 and 96 hours for induration, erythema, vesicles. (5) Treatment: Allergen avoidance; topical corticosteroids for mild reactions; systemic corticosteroids for severe reactions (e.g., extensive poison ivy).

Significance: Contact dermatitis illustrates the delayed, T cell-mediated nature of Type IV hypersensitivity. The 24-72 hour time course distinguishes it from immediate (Type I) reactions. Patch testing identifies specific allergens, enabling avoidance.

image.alt: Poison ivy contact dermatitis: linear vesicular rash. Mechanism: sensitization (first exposure) → T cell priming; elicitation (re-exposure) → T cell migration, cytokine release, inflammation. Patch testing.
image.caption: Contact Dermatitis: Type IV hypersensitivity. T cells sensitized by hapten (urushiol, nickel) cause delayed (24-72 hour) inflammation upon re-exposure. Patch testing identifies causative allergens.

classifications.type: Hypersensitivity: Type II (Antibody-Mediated Cytotoxic) and Type III (Immune Complex-Mediated)
classifications.value: Best for understanding autoimmune cytopenias, Goodpasture syndrome, Graves' disease (Type II), and systemic lupus erythematosus, serum sickness (Type III).
characteristics: Type II (Antibody-Mediated Cytotoxic): Mechanism—IgG or IgM antibodies bind cell surface or extracellular matrix antigens → complement activation (C3b opsonization, MAC lysis) → ADCC (NK cells via FcγRIII) → cellular dysfunction (receptor blockade or stimulation). Clinical syndromes: Autoimmune hemolytic anemia (anti-RBC antibodies), immune thrombocytopenia (anti-platelet antibodies), Goodpasture syndrome (anti-glomerular basement membrane antibodies), Graves' disease (stimulatory anti-TSH receptor antibodies), myasthenia gravis (blocking anti-ACh receptor antibodies), pemphigus vulgaris (anti-desmoglein antibodies).

Type III (Immune Complex-Mediated): Mechanism—Antigen-antibody complexes deposit in tissues (small vessels, glomeruli, joints) → complement activation (C3a, C5a) → neutrophil recruitment → release of lysosomal enzymes, reactive oxygen species → tissue injury. Clinical syndromes: Serum sickness (acute, following foreign protein administration), systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE, chronic immune complex disease), post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis, rheumatoid arthritis (some manifestations), vasculitis (some forms), drug-induced immune complex disease.
classifications.behavior:
Type II hypersensitivity involves antibodies directed against cell surface or tissue antigens. The clinical consequences depend on the antibody's action: (1) Opsonization and complement-mediated lysis—destruction of target cells (autoimmune hemolytic anemia, immune thrombocytopenia). (2) Antibody-dependent cellular cytotoxicity (ADCC)—NK cells kill antibody-coated cells. (3) Receptor blockade—antibodies block receptor function (myasthenia gravis: anti-AChR antibodies block neuromuscular transmission). (4) Receptor stimulation—antibodies activate receptors (Graves' disease: anti-TSHR antibodies stimulate thyroid hormone production). (5) Tissue damage—antibodies bind extracellular matrix (Goodpasture: anti-GBM antibodies cause glomerulonephritis and pulmonary hemorrhage).

Type III hypersensitivity involves deposition of preformed immune complexes. The size and composition of complexes determine where they deposit: large complexes are cleared by phagocytes; small complexes may deposit in vessel walls. Complement activation generates anaphylatoxins (C3a, C5a) that recruit neutrophils, which release tissue-damaging enzymes. Chronic immune complex deposition causes vasculitis, glomerulonephritis, and arthritis.

Clinical correlations: SLE is the prototypical Type III autoimmune disease, with immune complex deposition in glomeruli (nephritis), skin (rash), joints (arthritis), and vessels (vasculitis). Anti-dsDNA antibodies correlate with disease activity. Treatment includes corticosteroids, hydroxychloroquine, and immunosuppressants (mycophenolate, cyclophosphamide, belimumab—anti-BAFF). Serum sickness (fever, rash, arthralgias, lymphadenopathy) occurs 7-14 days after foreign protein administration (e.g., antithymocyte globulin, some monoclonal antibodies) and is treated with corticosteroids and antihistamines.

examples.process: Goodpasture Syndrome: Type II Anti-GBM Disease
examples.explanation:
Goodpasture syndrome is a classic Type II hypersensitivity disorder: (1) Autoantigen: α3 chain of type IV collagen in glomerular basement membrane (GBM) and alveolar basement membrane. (2) Antibodies: IgG antibodies (especially IgG1 subclass) bind to the non-collagenous domain (NC1) of α3(IV) collagen. (3) Mechanism: Antibody binding activates complement (classical pathway) → C3b opsonization, C5a chemotaxis → neutrophil infiltration → release of proteases, reactive oxygen species → glomerulonephritis and alveolar hemorrhage. (4) Clinical: Rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis (RPGN)—hematuria, proteinuria, renal failure; pulmonary hemorrhage—hemoptysis, dyspnea, diffuse alveolar infiltrates on imaging. (5) Diagnosis: Serum anti-GBM antibodies (ELISA); kidney biopsy—linear IgG staining along GBM (immunofluorescence). (6) Treatment: Plasmapheresis (removes pathogenic antibodies) + cyclophosphamide (suppresses antibody production) + corticosteroids (anti-inflammatory).

Significance: Goodpasture syndrome illustrates Type II antibody-mediated tissue injury. The linear immunofluorescence pattern (antibodies evenly distributed along GBM) distinguishes it from the granular pattern of Type III immune complex deposition in SLE.

image.alt: Goodpasture syndrome: anti-GBM antibodies bind α3(IV) collagen in glomerular and alveolar basement membranes. Complement activation, neutrophil recruitment → glomerulonephritis, pulmonary hemorrhage. Linear IgG staining on immunofluorescence.
image.caption: Goodpasture Syndrome: Type II hypersensitivity. Anti-glomerular basement membrane (GBM) antibodies cause rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis and pulmonary hemorrhage. Linear immunofluorescence is diagnostic.

examples.process: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus (SLE): Type III Immune Complex Disease
examples.explanation:
SLE is the prototypical Type III autoimmune disease: (1) Autoantibodies: Anti-nuclear antibodies (ANA), anti-dsDNA, anti-Smith, anti-Ro/SSA, anti-La/SSB. (2) Mechanism: Autoantibodies form immune complexes with nuclear antigens released from apoptotic cells; complexes deposit in small vessels (glomeruli, skin, joints, vessels). (3) Complement activation: C1q binds complexes → classical pathway activation → C3a, C5a recruit neutrophils → tissue damage. (4) Clinical: Constitutional—fever, fatigue; Musculoskeletal—arthralgias, arthritis; Mucocutaneous—malar rash, discoid rash, photosensitivity; Renal—lupus nephritis (proteinuria, hematuria, renal failure); Hematologic—hemolytic anemia, thrombocytopenia, leukopenia; Serositis—pleuritis, pericarditis; Neurologic—seizures, psychosis. (5) Diagnosis: ANA (high sensitivity), anti-dsDNA and anti-Smith (high specificity); renal biopsy shows immune complex deposition (granular IgG, C3 on immunofluorescence). (6) Treatment: Hydroxychloroquine (all patients); corticosteroids for flares; immunosuppressants (mycophenolate, cyclophosphamide) for nephritis; belimumab (anti-BAFF) for active disease; anifrolumab (anti-IFNAR) for moderate-severe SLE.

Significance: SLE exemplifies Type III immune complex-mediated disease. The granular immunofluorescence pattern (immune complexes deposited in irregular clumps) contrasts with the linear pattern of Type II diseases. Anti-dsDNA titers correlate with disease activity, particularly nephritis.

image.alt: SLE pathogenesis: autoantibodies (ANA, anti-dsDNA) form immune complexes with nuclear antigens; complexes deposit in glomeruli, skin, vessels; complement activation → inflammation. Clinical: malar rash, arthritis, nephritis. Granular immunofluorescence.
image.caption: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus (SLE): Type III immune complex disease. Autoantibodies form complexes that deposit in tissues, activating complement and causing multi-organ inflammation (kidney, skin, joints, serosa).

classifications.value: Best for understanding the spectrum of autoimmune diseases, their mechanisms, and treatment approaches.
characteristics: Organ-Specific Autoimmune Diseases: Target antigens confined to specific organs. Examples: Hashimoto's thyroiditis (thyroid peroxidase, thyroglobulin)—hypothyroidism; Graves' disease (TSH receptor)—hyperthyroidism; Type 1 diabetes (insulin, GAD65, IA-2)—pancreatic β-cell destruction; Multiple sclerosis (myelin antigens)—CNS demyelination; Myasthenia gravis (ACh receptor)—neuromuscular junction; Pemphigus vulgaris (desmogleins)—skin blistering; Autoimmune hepatitis (liver antigens)—hepatocellular inflammation.

Systemic Autoimmune Diseases: Target antigens widely distributed; multi-organ involvement. Examples: Systemic lupus erythematosus (nuclear antigens)—skin, joints, kidney, serosa, hematologic; Rheumatoid arthritis (rheumatoid factor, anti-CCP)—synovial joints, systemic inflammation; Systemic sclerosis (scleroderma, anti-centromere, anti-Scl-70)—skin, lungs, GI tract, vessels; Sjögren's syndrome (anti-Ro/SSA, anti-La/SSB)—exocrine glands (dry eyes, dry mouth), systemic; Antiphospholipid syndrome (anti-cardiolipin, lupus anticoagulant)—thrombosis, pregnancy loss; Polymyositis/dermatomyositis (anti-Jo-1, anti-Mi-2)—muscle, skin.

Mechanisms of Autoimmunity: (1) Failure of central tolerance (thymic negative selection)—AIRE deficiency causes autoimmune polyendocrine syndrome (APS-1). (2) Failure of peripheral tolerance (Treg dysfunction)—IPEX syndrome (FoxP3 mutations). (3) Molecular mimicry—cross-reactive antigens: Group A streptococcal M protein and cardiac myosin (rheumatic fever); Campylobacter jejuni and gangliosides (Guillain-Barré). (4) Epitope spreading—immune response spreads from initial epitope to other epitopes on same or different antigens. (5) Genetic susceptibility—HLA associations: HLA-DR4 (rheumatoid arthritis), HLA-DR3/DR4 (type 1 diabetes), HLA-DQ2/DQ8 (celiac disease), HLA-DR15 (multiple sclerosis). (6) Environmental triggers—infections, smoking, UV light, drugs.
classifications.behavior:
Autoimmune diseases result from complex interactions between genetic susceptibility and environmental triggers. The presence of autoantibodies may precede clinical disease by years (e.g., anti-dsDNA in SLE, anti-CCP in RA). Disease pathogenesis involves both antibody-mediated and T cell-mediated mechanisms.

Organ-specific autoimmune diseases often result from T cell-mediated destruction (type 1 diabetes: CD8+ T cells kill β-cells) or antibody-mediated dysfunction (Graves': stimulatory antibodies; myasthenia: blocking antibodies). Treatment typically involves hormone replacement (thyroid hormone, insulin) or symptomatic therapy, with immunosuppression for severe cases.

Systemic autoimmune diseases involve multiple immune mechanisms and affect multiple organs. Treatment is based on disease activity and organ involvement: antimalarials (hydroxychloroquine) for mild SLE; methotrexate or TNF inhibitors for rheumatoid arthritis; mycophenolate or cyclophosphamide for lupus nephritis; rituximab (anti-CD20) for refractory autoimmune diseases.

Clinical correlations: Autoimmune diseases are more common in women (female:male ratio 9:1 in SLE, 3:1 in RA). Overlap syndromes occur where patients meet criteria for multiple autoimmune diseases. Autoimmune disease risk is increased in first-degree relatives, reflecting shared genetic susceptibility.

examples.explanation:
Rheumatoid arthritis is a systemic autoimmune disease primarily affecting synovial joints: (1) Pathogenesis: Genetic susceptibility (HLA-DR4, PTPN22, etc.) + environmental trigger (smoking, etc.) → loss of tolerance to citrullinated proteins. (2) Autoantibodies: Rheumatoid factor (RF, anti-IgG Fc) and anti-citrullinated protein antibodies (ACPA, highly specific) appear years before clinical disease. (3) Immune activation: ACPAs form immune complexes with citrullinated antigens in synovium → complement activation → neutrophil and macrophage infiltration → release of TNF, IL-1, IL-6, MMPs. (4) Synovial hyperplasia (pannus) invades cartilage and bone → joint erosion. (5) Clinical: Symmetric inflammatory polyarthritis (small joints of hands/feet), morning stiffness (>1 hour), systemic inflammation (fatigue, weight loss). (6) Extra-articular: Rheumatoid nodules, vasculitis, interstitial lung disease, cardiovascular disease. (7) Diagnosis: ≥1 swollen joint, RF or ACPA positive, elevated acute phase reactants (ESR, CRP). (8) Treatment: DMARDs: methotrexate (anchor drug); biologics: TNF inhibitors (etanercept, adalimumab, infliximab), IL-6 inhibitor (tocilizumab), CTLA-4-Ig (abatacept), anti-CD20 (rituximab); JAK inhibitors (tofacitinib, upadacitinib).

Significance: RA illustrates the transition from preclinical autoimmunity (autoantibodies) to clinical disease (synovitis). Targeted biologics have transformed outcomes, with early aggressive treatment leading to remission in many patients.

image.alt: RA pathogenesis: ACPA antibodies target citrullinated proteins in synovium → immune complex deposition → complement activation → TNF, IL-1, IL-6 → pannus formation, cartilage/bone erosion. Clinical: symmetric small joint arthritis, rheumatoid nodules.
image.caption: Rheumatoid Arthritis: Autoimmune synovitis mediated by ACPA antibodies and pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF, IL-6). Targeted biologics (TNF inhibitors, IL-6 inhibitors) have revolutionized treatment.

classifications.value: Best for understanding the patterns of infection that suggest specific immune defects and the diagnostic and therapeutic approaches.
characteristics: Primary Immunodeficiencies (PIDs): Genetic defects in immune development or function. Categories: B cell deficiencies (X-linked agammaglobulinemia—BTK mutation; common variable immunodeficiency—CVID; selective IgA deficiency)—recurrent encapsulated bacterial infections (Streptococcus pneumoniae, Haemophilus influenzae). T cell deficiencies (DiGeorge syndrome—22q11.2 deletion; severe combined immunodeficiency—SCID, multiple genes)—opportunistic infections (Pneumocystis jirovecii, fungi, viruses). Combined deficiencies (SCID, Wiskott-Aldrich syndrome)—severe, early-onset infections. Phagocyte deficiencies (chronic granulomatous disease—CGD, NADPH oxidase; leukocyte adhesion deficiency—LAD)—catalase-positive bacteria (Staphylococcus aureus, Serratia, Burkholderia, Nocardia), fungi (Aspergillus). Complement deficiencies—encapsulated bacteria (C3 deficiency), Neisseria (C5-C9 deficiency). Innate immune deficiencies (TLR3, UNC93B1)—HSV encephalitis; STAT3 mutations (hyper-IgE syndrome)—staphylococcal abscesses, fungal infections.

Secondary Immunodeficiencies: Acquired defects. HIV/AIDS—CD4+ T cell depletion → opportunistic infections (PJP, candidiasis, cryptococcosis, toxoplasmosis, MAC), malignancies (Kaposi sarcoma, lymphoma). Malnutrition—protein-calorie deficiency → impaired immune function. Iatrogenic—chemotherapy, radiation, immunosuppressive drugs (corticosteroids, calcineurin inhibitors, biologics). Splenectomy—increased risk of encapsulated bacterial infections (post-splenectomy sepsis). Age-related—immunosenescence (increased infections, decreased vaccine responses).
classifications.behavior:
Primary immunodeficiencies (PIDs) present with recurrent, severe, or unusual infections, often in infancy or childhood. The pattern of infections provides clues to the underlying defect: B cell deficiencies → recurrent sinopulmonary infections with encapsulated bacteria; T cell deficiencies → opportunistic infections (PJP, fungi, viruses), failure to thrive; combined deficiencies → severe infections in first months of life; phagocyte deficiencies → deep-seated abscesses, granulomas, catalase-positive organisms; complement deficiencies → encapsulated bacteria (C3) or Neisseria (C5-C9).

Diagnosis of PIDs involves: (1) Screening: CBC with differential (lymphopenia, neutropenia), quantitative immunoglobulins (IgG, IgA, IgM), vaccine titers (response to protein and polysaccharide vaccines), HIV testing. (2) Flow cytometry: lymphocyte subsets (CD3, CD4, CD8, CD19, CD16/56). (3) Functional assays: DHR for CGD (respiratory burst), CH50/AH50 for complement. (4) Genetic testing for definitive diagnosis.

Secondary immunodeficiencies are more common than primary. HIV/AIDS is the most significant globally; CD4+ T cell count guides prophylaxis and treatment (antiretroviral therapy restores immune function). Splenectomy patients require vaccination (pneumococcal, meningococcal, Hib) and daily antibiotic prophylaxis for children.

Clinical correlations: Newborn screening for SCID (T cell receptor excision circles—TREC) is now universal in many countries, enabling early diagnosis and hematopoietic stem cell transplantation (HSCT) before infections occur. Gene therapy is approved for several PIDs (SCID-X1, ADA-SCID, X-linked CGD). IVIG replacement is standard for antibody deficiencies (XLA, CVID).

examples.process: Severe Combined Immunodeficiency (SCID): The Prototypical Combined Immunodeficiency
examples.explanation:
SCID is a group of genetic disorders characterized by absence of T cells and variable B and NK cell defects: (1) Mechanisms: X-linked SCID (IL2RG mutation)—common γ chain deficiency → absent T cells, absent NK cells, non-functional B cells (T-B+ NK-). Adenosine deaminase (ADA) deficiency—toxic metabolite accumulation → absent T, B, NK cells (T-B- NK-). RAG deficiency—failure of V(D)J recombination → absent T, B cells (T-B- NK+). Artemis deficiency—DNA repair defect → radiosensitive SCID. (2) Clinical: Infants present in first months of life with failure to thrive, chronic diarrhea, thrush (Candida), PJP pneumonia, disseminated BCG (if vaccinated), severe viral infections (CMV, RSV, varicella). (3) Diagnosis: Lymphopenia (ALC <2,500/μL); absent or markedly reduced T cells on flow cytometry; low TRECs on newborn screening; genetic testing. (4) Treatment: Definitive: hematopoietic stem cell transplantation (HSCT) from HLA-matched sibling (90% survival) or unrelated donor; gene therapy for X-SCID and ADA-SCID. Supportive: IVIG (antibody replacement), antimicrobial prophylaxis (TMP-SMX for PJP), avoid live vaccines, strict infection prevention.

Significance: SCID is a pediatric emergency—delays in diagnosis lead to death from infections before HSCT can be performed. Newborn screening for SCID (TREC assay) has dramatically improved outcomes by enabling diagnosis before infections develop.

image.alt: SCID mechanisms: IL2RG (X-linked) → absent T and NK cells; ADA deficiency → absent T, B, NK cells; RAG deficiency → absent T and B cells. Clinical: early-onset infections (PJP, Candida, viruses). Newborn screening (TREC) enables early diagnosis.
image.caption: Severe Combined Immunodeficiency (SCID): Genetic defects causing absence of T cells (and variable B/NK cells) lead to severe, early-onset infections. Newborn screening (TREC) and early HSCT are life-saving.

examples.explanation:
HIV/AIDS is the most common severe secondary immunodeficiency globally: (1) Pathogenesis: HIV infects CD4+ T cells (via CD4 and co-receptor CCR5/CXCR4) → viral replication → CD4+ T cell depletion → progressive immune deficiency. (2) Natural history: Acute infection (flu-like) → latency (CD4 count stable for years) → AIDS (CD4 <200/μL or opportunistic infection). (3) Opportunistic infections: CD4 <200: Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia (PJP), esophageal candidiasis; CD4 <100: Toxoplasma gondii, Cryptococcus neoformans, disseminated Mycobacterium avium complex (MAC); CD4 <50: CMV retinitis, disseminated MAC. (4) Malignancies: Kaposi sarcoma (HHV-8), non-Hodgkin lymphoma, cervical cancer. (5) Diagnosis: HIV antibody test (4th generation ELISA); confirm with Western blot or HIV RNA. CD4 count and HIV viral load guide treatment and prognosis. (6) Treatment: Antiretroviral therapy (ART)—combination of drugs from different classes (NRTIs, NNRTIs, protease inhibitors, integrase inhibitors). ART restores CD4 count (immune reconstitution) and suppresses viral load to undetectable. (7) Prophylaxis: PJP prophylaxis (TMP-SMX) when CD4 <200/μL; MAC prophylaxis (azithromycin) when CD4 <50/μL.

Significance: HIV/AIDS illustrates the clinical consequences of progressive CD4+ T cell depletion. With effective ART, HIV has become a chronic manageable disease; life expectancy approaches that of uninfected individuals. Prevention includes pre-exposure prophylaxis (PrEP) and post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP).

image.alt: HIV pathogenesis: virus infects CD4+ T cells → CD4 depletion → opportunistic infections (PJP, MAC, Toxoplasma) as CD4 declines. ART suppresses viral load, restores CD4. PJP prophylaxis when CD4 <200.
image.caption: HIV/AIDS: Progressive CD4+ T cell depletion leads to opportunistic infections. Antiretroviral therapy (ART) restores immune function and prevents disease progression.

principles.principle: Hypersensitivity Reactions: Mechanism Determines Clinical Presentation and Treatment
principles.statement: The four types of hypersensitivity are distinguished by their immune mechanism (antibody vs. cell-mediated), time course, and effector pathways—determining clinical manifestations and guiding therapeutic approaches.
principles.application:
Functional inference: (1) Type I (IgE/mast cell) responds to antihistamines, epinephrine, and anti-IgE biologics (omalizumab). (2) Type II (antibody-mediated) responds to immunosuppression, plasmapheresis (removing antibodies), and anti-CD20 (rituximab) to deplete B cells. (3) Type III (immune complex) responds to immunosuppression and treatments that reduce antigen burden. (4) Type IV (T cell-mediated) responds to corticosteroids, calcineurin inhibitors (tacrolimus), and biologics targeting T cell cytokines (anti-TNF, anti-IL-17).

Clinical application: Recognizing the hypersensitivity type guides diagnosis and treatment: a patient with acute urticaria after penicillin (Type I) requires antihistamines and epinephrine if systemic; a patient with autoimmune hemolytic anemia (Type II) may respond to corticosteroids and rituximab; a patient with SLE (Type III) requires immunosuppression based on organ involvement; a patient with contact dermatitis (Type IV) requires allergen avoidance and topical corticosteroids.
principles.example:
Drug hypersensitivity: Different mechanisms cause different reactions to the same drug. (1) Penicillin: Type I (anaphylaxis) → IgE-mediated, occurs within minutes after prior sensitization; Type II (hemolytic anemia) → antibody-mediated destruction of RBCs; Type III (serum sickness) → immune complex deposition; Type IV (maculopapular rash) → T cell-mediated, occurs days after exposure. (2) Implications: Prior history of anaphylaxis (Type I) is an absolute contraindication; history of non-severe rash (Type IV) may not preclude use if necessary. (3) Evaluation: Skin testing for IgE-mediated reactions; patch testing for delayed reactions.

Significance: Understanding that the same drug can cause different hypersensitivity reactions by different mechanisms is critical for safe prescribing and accurate diagnosis of adverse drug reactions.
principles.analogy: Hypersensitivity types are like different fire-starting mechanisms: Type I is a match (IgE, immediate); Type II is a chemical reaction (antibody-mediated); Type III is a slow burn (immune complex deposition); Type IV is embers that ignite later (T cell-mediated). Each requires a different firefighting approach.

image.alt: Penicillin hypersensitivity mechanisms: Type I (anaphylaxis, IgE-mediated), Type II (hemolytic anemia, antibody-mediated), Type III (serum sickness, immune complex), Type IV (maculopapular rash, T cell-mediated).
image.caption: Drug Hypersensitivity: The same drug (penicillin) can cause all four types of hypersensitivity reactions. Mechanism determines clinical presentation, severity, and management.

principles.statement: Autoimmune diseases result from failure of central tolerance (thymic negative selection) or peripheral tolerance (Treg dysfunction, molecular mimicry, epitope spreading), with genetic susceptibility (HLA associations) and environmental triggers.
principles.application:
Functional inference: (1) Central tolerance failure: AIRE deficiency → impaired negative selection → multiple autoimmune targets (APS-1). (2) Peripheral tolerance failure: FoxP3 mutations → Treg deficiency → IPEX syndrome (enteropathy, endocrinopathy, eczema). (3) Molecular mimicry: Streptococcus pyogenes M protein cross-reacts with cardiac myosin → rheumatic heart disease. (4) Epitope spreading: Initial immune response to one epitope spreads to other self-epitopes, amplifying autoimmunity.

Clinical application: Understanding mechanisms guides prevention and treatment: HLA typing can identify risk (e.g., HLA-DQ2/DQ8 for celiac disease; diagnosis requires confirmation). Treatments targeting central tolerance pathways are not available; peripheral tolerance can be enhanced with Treg-based therapies (in development). Molecular mimicry underscores the importance of treating infections that may trigger autoimmunity (e.g., antibiotic treatment of strep pharyngitis to prevent rheumatic fever).
principles.example:
Celiac disease: Autoimmunity triggered by environmental antigen. (1) Genetics: HLA-DQ2 (90%) or HLA-DQ8 (remaining) required but not sufficient. (2) Trigger: Gluten (gliadin) ingestion in genetically susceptible individuals. (3) Mechanism: Gluten peptides deamidated by tissue transglutaminase (tTG), become highly immunogenic; presented on HLA-DQ2/DQ8 to CD4+ T cells; T cells drive immune response, producing anti-tTG antibodies; CD8+ T cells cause enterocyte destruction. (4) Clinical: Diarrhea, malabsorption, weight loss, dermatitis herpetiformis. (5) Diagnosis: Anti-tTG IgA antibodies (high sensitivity); duodenal biopsy (villous atrophy, intraepithelial lymphocytosis). (6) Treatment: Lifelong gluten-free diet restores mucosal architecture and prevents complications (lymphoma, osteoporosis).

Significance: Celiac disease illustrates the interplay of genetics (HLA), environmental trigger (gluten), and autoimmunity (anti-tTG antibodies). The causative antigen is known, enabling specific dietary therapy.
principles.analogy: Autoimmunity is like a security system that mistakes residents for intruders. Central tolerance is the initial programming that teaches the system who is "resident." Peripheral tolerance is ongoing surveillance that prevents mistaken attacks. When these fail, the system attacks the house itself.

image.alt: Celiac disease pathogenesis: gluten ingestion in HLA-DQ2/DQ8 individuals → deamidation by tTG → presentation to CD4+ T cells → anti-tTG antibodies, CD8+ T cell-mediated enterocyte destruction → villous atrophy. Gluten-free diet restores mucosa.
image.caption: Celiac Disease: Autoimmunity triggered by gluten in genetically susceptible individuals (HLA-DQ2/DQ8). Gluten-free diet is effective treatment, demonstrating the principle of removing the environmental trigger.

principles.principle: Immunodeficiency: Infection Pattern Localizes the Defect
principles.statement: The types of infections that occur in immunodeficient patients reflect the specific immune component that is compromised—encapsulated bacteria (B cell defects), opportunistic infections (T cell defects), catalase-positive organisms (phagocyte defects), Neisseria (complement defects)—enabling targeted diagnostic evaluation.
principles.application:
Functional inference: (1) B cell defects → recurrent sinopulmonary infections with encapsulated bacteria (S. pneumoniae, H. influenzae). (2) T cell defects → opportunistic infections: PJP, Candida, CMV, EBV, Cryptococcus, Toxoplasma, mycobacteria. (3) Combined defects → severe, early-onset infections with both bacterial and opportunistic pathogens. (4) Phagocyte defects → deep abscesses, granulomas, catalase-positive bacteria (Staph. aureus, Serratia, Burkholderia, Nocardia), Aspergillus. (5) Complement defects → encapsulated bacteria (C3 deficiency) or Neisseria (C5-C9 deficiency).

Clinical application: Infection history guides initial laboratory evaluation: recurrent pneumonia with encapsulated bacteria → quantitative immunoglobulins, vaccine titers; PJP in adult → HIV test; deep abscesses and Aspergillus → DHR for CGD; recurrent Neisseria → CH50, AH50. This pattern recognition enables efficient diagnosis.
principles.example:
Recurrent Neisseria meningitidis infections: (1) Case: 20-year-old with second episode of meningococcal meningitis; no risk factors (no asplenia, no immunosuppression). (2) Localization: Neisseria infections in the absence of other infections suggest terminal complement deficiency (C5-C9). (3) Mechanism: MAC (C5b-9) is required for lysis of Gram-negative bacteria; deficiency prevents MAC formation, allowing Neisseria to survive despite antibody opsonization. (4) Evaluation: CH50 (total complement activity) → absent or markedly reduced; specific component assays → deficiency in C5, C6, C7, C8, or C9. (5) Management: Vaccination (meningococcal conjugate and serogroup B vaccines), prophylactic antibiotics, prompt treatment of infections. (6) Family screening: Complement deficiencies are often inherited; screen first-degree relatives.

Significance: Recurrent Neisseria infections point specifically to terminal complement deficiency, demonstrating how infection pattern localizes immune defect.
principles.analogy: Immunodeficiency diagnosis is like detective work: The pattern of infections (clues) points to the broken immune component (culprit). Encapsulated bacteria point to B cells; opportunistic infections point to T cells; catalase-positive organisms point to phagocytes; Neisseria point to complement. Each clue narrows the investigation.

image.alt: Infection patterns by immune defect: B cell defects (encapsulated bacteria: S. pneumoniae, H. influenzae); T cell defects (opportunistic: PJP, Candida, CMV); phagocyte defects (catalase-positive: Staph, Aspergillus); complement defects (Neisseria).
image.caption: Immunodeficiency Patterns: The type of infection suggests the underlying immune defect. This pattern recognition guides targeted diagnostic evaluation and management.

components.definition: Hypersensitivity: IgE, mast cells, basophils (Type I); IgG/IgM, complement, Fc receptors (Type II); immune complexes, complement, neutrophils (Type III); T cells, macrophages, cytokines (Type IV). Autoimmunity: autoantibodies, autoreactive T cells, HLA associations, environmental triggers. Immunodeficiency: genetic defects, CD4 count, immunoglobulins, complement levels, neutrophil function.
components.equation: CD4 count thresholds in HIV/AIDS: <500/μL (increased infection risk), <200/μL (AIDS, PJP prophylaxis), <100/μL (Toxoplasma, MAC prophylaxis), <50/μL (CMV retinitis, disseminated MAC).
components.interpretation: CD4 count is a critical biomarker in HIV/AIDS, correlating with risk of specific opportunistic infections. Prophylaxis thresholds are based on CD4 count, preventing infections before they occur. Immune reconstitution with ART restores CD4 count and reduces infection risk.

image.alt: Summary diagram: Hypersensitivity mechanisms (Types I-IV); autoimmune disease examples (RA, SLE, T1D); immunodeficiency categories (B cell, T cell, phagocyte, complement).
image.caption: Key Components of Immune Disorders: Hypersensitivity types differ by immune mechanism; autoimmune diseases affect specific organs or systemic; immunodeficiencies involve distinct immune components.

categories.type: Biologics in Immune Disorders: Targeted Therapies
categories.description: Biologic therapies (monoclonal antibodies, fusion proteins) target specific immune pathways, revolutionizing treatment of hypersensitivity, autoimmune diseases, and immunodeficiencies.
categories.detailed_mechanism:
Anti-IgE (omalizumab): Binds free IgE, prevents binding to FcεRI on mast cells/basophils → used in allergic asthma, chronic urticaria. Anti-IL-5 (mepolizumab, reslizumab): Blocks IL-5, reduces eosinophil production → eosinophilic asthma, eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis (EGPA). Anti-IL-4Rα (dupilumab): Blocks IL-4 and IL-13 signaling → atopic dermatitis, asthma, nasal polyps.

Anti-TNF (infliximab, adalimumab, etanercept): Neutralizes TNF-α → rheumatoid arthritis, inflammatory bowel disease, psoriasis, ankylosing spondylitis. Anti-IL-6R (tocilizumab): Blocks IL-6 signaling → rheumatoid arthritis, cytokine release syndrome, giant cell arteritis. Anti-CD20 (rituximab): Depletes B cells → rheumatoid arthritis, ANCA-associated vasculitis, SLE, autoimmune hemolytic anemia, immune thrombocytopenia. CTLA-4-Ig (abatacept): Blocks CD28 co-stimulation → rheumatoid arthritis, juvenile idiopathic arthritis.

Anti-BAFF (belimumab): Blocks B cell survival factor → SLE. Anti-IFNAR (anifrolumab): Blocks type I interferon receptor → SLE.

Immunoglobulin replacement (IVIG, SCIG): Provides polyclonal IgG → primary antibody deficiencies, secondary hypogammaglobulinemia, immune thrombocytopenia, Guillain-Barré syndrome.

examples.process: Rituximab: B Cell Depletion in Autoimmune Disease
examples.explanation:
Rituximab is a chimeric monoclonal antibody targeting CD20 on B cells: (1) Mechanism: Binds CD20 on B cells (not plasma cells) → induces ADCC, complement-dependent cytotoxicity, and direct apoptosis → B cell depletion (peripheral B cells reduced for 6-12 months). (2) Indications: Rheumatoid arthritis (with methotrexate); ANCA-associated vasculitis (granulomatosis with polyangiitis, microscopic polyangiitis); pemphigus vulgaris; immune thrombocytopenia (off-label); autoimmune hemolytic anemia (off-label); SLE (off-label). (3) Efficacy: In ANCA-associated vasculitis, rituximab is non-inferior to cyclophosphamide for induction and superior for maintenance; in rheumatoid arthritis, effective in patients with inadequate response to TNF inhibitors. (4) Adverse effects: Infusion reactions; prolonged B cell depletion (increased infection risk, particularly encapsulated bacteria); hypogammaglobulinemia; reactivation of hepatitis B; progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy (PML, rare). (5) Monitoring: CD19/20 counts, immunoglobulins; HBV screening before initiation; infection prophylaxis as indicated.

Significance: Rituximab exemplifies targeted biologic therapy—depleting the B cell compartment to treat autoantibody-mediated diseases. Understanding the mechanism (B cell depletion, plasma cells spared) explains the therapeutic effect and risks (hypogammaglobulinemia, infection risk).

image.alt: Rituximab mechanism: anti-CD20 monoclonal antibody binds CD20 on B cells → ADCC, complement activation, apoptosis → B cell depletion. Indications: RA, ANCA vasculitis, pemphigus. Monitoring: CD19, immunoglobulins.
image.caption: Rituximab: Anti-CD20 monoclonal antibody depletes B cells, treating autoantibody-mediated diseases (ANCA vasculitis, rheumatoid arthritis, pemphigus). Mechanism explains efficacy and infection risks.

categories.description: Diagnosis of immune disorders relies on a combination of clinical pattern recognition, laboratory testing, and specialized immunologic assays.
categories.detailed_mechanism:
**Hypersensitivity**: Skin prick testing (Type I)—allergen-specific IgE; intradermal testing; patch testing (Type IV)—contact allergens; serum-specific IgE; tryptase (mast cell degranulation, anaphylaxis).

**Autoimmunity**: Autoantibody panels—ANA (SLE), anti-dsDNA (SLE nephritis), anti-Smith (SLE-specific), anti-CCP (RA-specific), rheumatoid factor (non-specific), anti-TPO (Hashimoto's), anti-GAD65 (type 1 diabetes), anti-AChR (myasthenia gravis). Complement levels (C3, C4)—low in SLE (consumption). Inflammatory markers (ESR, CRP).

**Immunodeficiency**: CBC with differential (lymphopenia, neutropenia); quantitative immunoglobulins (IgG, IgA, IgM); vaccine titers (response to protein vaccines—tetanus, diphtheria; polysaccharide vaccines—pneumococcal); flow cytometry (lymphocyte subsets—CD3, CD4, CD8, CD19, CD16/56); DHR assay (CGD, respiratory burst); CH50, AH50 (complement function); HIV testing; genetic testing (targeted or whole exome).

**Imaging**: CT for lymphadenopathy, organomegaly, pulmonary infections; MRI for CNS involvement.

examples.explanation:
SLE diagnosis uses autoantibody testing with the 2019 EULAR/ACR classification criteria: (1) Entry criterion: ANA ≥1:80 (required). (2) Additive criteria: Clinical (constitutional, hematologic, neuropsychiatric, mucocutaneous, serosal, musculoskeletal, renal) and immunologic (anti-dsDNA, anti-Smith, anti-phospholipid antibodies, low complement, direct Coombs test). (3) Anti-dsDNA: Highly specific for SLE (>95%); titers correlate with disease activity, especially nephritis. (4) Anti-Smith (Sm): Highly specific (>99%) but less sensitive (~30%). (5) Anti-Ro/SSA, anti-La/SSB: Associated with Sjögren's syndrome, neonatal lupus, subacute cutaneous lupus. (6) Anti-phospholipid antibodies: Anti-cardiolipin, anti-β2-glycoprotein I, lupus anticoagulant; associated with thrombosis, pregnancy loss.

Significance: Autoantibody panels are diagnostic and prognostic in SLE. Anti-dsDNA titers guide treatment decisions (rising titers may herald flare). Understanding antibody specificity allows accurate diagnosis and monitoring.

image.alt: SLE autoantibodies: ANA (entry criterion), anti-dsDNA (specific, correlates with nephritis), anti-Smith (highly specific), anti-Ro/SSA (neonatal lupus, Sjögren's), anti-phospholipid (thrombosis).
image.caption: Autoantibody Testing in SLE: ANA is sensitive but not specific; anti-dsDNA and anti-Smith are highly specific. Antibody profiles guide diagnosis, prognosis, and monitoring.

sections.section_title: Worked Example: Immune Disorder Case Analysis

worked_examples.title: Immune Disorder Case Analysis: Suspected Immunodeficiency
worked_examples.problem: A 3-year-old boy presents with recurrent sinopulmonary infections since infancy: six episodes of pneumonia (two requiring hospitalization), chronic otitis media, and persistent diarrhea. Immunizations: received routine vaccines including MMR, varicella, and rotavirus. Examination: small stature, no tonsils visible. Family history: maternal uncle died of pneumonia at age 2. (1) What type of immunodeficiency is most likely? (2) What diagnostic tests should be ordered? (3) Why are live vaccines a concern? (4) What is the treatment?

step_by_step.explanation: Interpret infection pattern and physical findings.
step_by_step.calculation: Recurrent sinopulmonary infections (pneumonia, otitis media) with encapsulated bacteria (S. pneumoniae, H. influenzae) → suggests B cell/antibody deficiency. Absent tonsils (lymphoid tissue hypoplasia), family history of early childhood death (X-linked pattern), onset in infancy. Pattern is consistent with X-linked agammaglobulinemia (XLA, Bruton's tyrosine kinase—BTK deficiency).

XLA is caused by mutations in BTK (Xq22), leading to failure of B cell development (block at pre-B cell stage). Results: absence of mature B cells, profound hypogammaglobulinemia (low IgG, IgA, IgM). No antibody production; T cell numbers and function are normal. Infections are due to encapsulated bacteria (require opsonizing antibodies).

step_by_step.explanation: Identify diagnostic approach and safety concerns.
step_by_step.calculation: Diagnostic tests: (1) Quantitative immunoglobulins (IgG, IgA, IgM) → profoundly decreased. (2) Lymphocyte subsets (flow cytometry) → absent or markedly decreased CD19+ B cells; normal T cells (CD3, CD4, CD8). (3) Vaccine titers → lack of protective antibodies to tetanus, diphtheria, pneumococcus (despite vaccination). (4) Genetic testing for BTK mutations. (5) Concern: Patient received live vaccines (MMR, varicella, rotavirus) before diagnosis. In antibody deficiency, live vaccines are generally not contraindicated (T cells are intact), but caution is needed. However, if combined immunodeficiency were present, live vaccines could cause disseminated infection. In this case, the pattern suggests isolated B cell deficiency, so vaccine history is less concerning but requires monitoring.

Treatment: Immunoglobulin replacement (IVIG or SCIG) every 3-4 weeks to maintain IgG trough >500-700 mg/dL; prevents sinopulmonary infections. Antimicrobial prophylaxis (TMP-SMX or azithromycin) may be used. Avoid live vaccines in the future (though not strictly contraindicated in isolated antibody deficiency, guidelines vary). Family counseling: X-linked inheritance; carrier testing for female relatives; prenatal diagnosis available. Prognosis: With immunoglobulin replacement, normal life expectancy; without treatment, death from infection by early adulthood.

worked_examples.final_answer: (1) Most likely diagnosis: X-linked agammaglobulinemia (XLA, Bruton's agammaglobulinemia)—a primary B cell immunodeficiency. (2) Diagnostic tests: Quantitative immunoglobulins (low IgG, IgA, IgM); flow cytometry (absent B cells); vaccine titers (lack of response); BTK gene sequencing. (3) Live vaccines (MMR, varicella, rotavirus) are generally not contraindicated in isolated B cell deficiency (T cells intact), but caution is needed; in combined immunodeficiency they are contraindicated. (4) Treatment: Immunoglobulin replacement (IVIG/SCIG); antimicrobial prophylaxis; family counseling; avoid live vaccines in the future.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Infection pattern (recurrent encapsulated bacterial infections) localizes the defect to B cells/antibody production. Absent tonsils and X-linked family history point to XLA. Diagnosis requires immunoglobulin quantification, B cell enumeration, and genetic confirmation. Treatment (IVIG) replaces the missing antibodies, preventing infections.

sections.section_title: Process Flow: Immune Complex Deposition in Type III Hypersensitivity
sections.introduction: Type III hypersensitivity results from deposition of antigen-antibody complexes in tissues, triggering complement activation and neutrophil-mediated inflammation. This flow illustrates the pathogenesis of immune complex diseases like SLE and serum sickness.

process_flow.description: Antigen (self or foreign) combines with antibody (IgG or IgM) to form immune complexes in circulation.
process_flow.mechanism: Antigen (e.g., nuclear antigens in SLE, foreign protein in serum sickness) and antibody (autoantibody or anti-foreign protein antibody) form complexes. Complex size depends on antigen:antibody ratio; small complexes (slight antigen excess) are most pathogenic.

image.alt: Antigen and antibody combine to form immune complexes; small complexes (slight antigen excess) are most pathogenic.
image.caption: Step 1: Immune Complex Formation. Antigen (self or foreign) binds antibody, forming immune complexes. Complex size and composition influence deposition and pathogenicity.

process_flow.description: Immune complexes deposit in vessel walls, glomeruli, joints, and other tissues due to hemodynamic factors and impaired clearance.
process_flow.mechanism: Complexes deposit in areas of increased vascular permeability and turbulent flow: glomeruli (mesangial, subendothelial, subepithelial), small vessels (skin, joints), choroid plexus. Factors: size, charge, complement activation, and clearance via erythrocyte CR1 (reduced in SLE).

image.alt: Immune complexes deposit in glomerular basement membrane (subendothelial, mesangial), small vessel walls, and synovium. Hemodynamic factors and impaired clearance contribute.
image.caption: Step 2: Complex Deposition. Immune complexes deposit in tissues where blood flow is turbulent (glomeruli, vessel walls, joints). Deposition is enhanced by increased vascular permeability and impaired clearance.

process_flow.description: Deposited complexes activate complement, generating anaphylatoxins and recruiting neutrophils.
process_flow.mechanism: C1q binds immune complexes (classical pathway) → activation of complement cascade → C3a and C5a (anaphylatoxins) → mast cell degranulation, increased vascular permeability; C5a is a potent neutrophil chemoattractant → neutrophils migrate to deposition site.

image.alt: Immune complexes activate complement: C1q binding initiates classical pathway → C3a, C5a generation → neutrophil chemotaxis; C5a activates neutrophils.
image.caption: Step 3: Complement Activation. Immune complexes activate the classical complement pathway, generating anaphylatoxins (C3a, C5a) that recruit and activate neutrophils.

process_flow.description: Neutrophils release lysosomal enzymes and reactive oxygen species, causing tissue damage.
process_flow.mechanism: Neutrophils bind immune complexes via Fc receptors and complement receptors → phagocytosis attempted but complexes are fixed → frustrated phagocytosis → release of proteases (elastase, collagenase), reactive oxygen species (respiratory burst), and pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF, IL-1) → tissue necrosis, vasculitis, glomerulonephritis.

image.alt: Neutrophils attempt to phagocytose immune complexes (frustrated phagocytosis), releasing proteases, ROS, and cytokines → tissue damage, vasculitis, glomerular injury.
image.caption: Step 4: Neutrophil-Mediated Injury. Neutrophils attempt to phagocytose fixed immune complexes, releasing tissue-damaging enzymes and reactive oxygen species, causing vasculitis, glomerulonephritis, and arthritis.

sections.section_title: Advanced Concepts: Immunogenetics and Precision Medicine
sections.definition: Advances in genetics and molecular immunology are enabling precision medicine approaches to immune disorders: genetic diagnosis of primary immunodeficiencies, pharmacogenomics to predict drug responses, and targeted biologics based on disease mechanisms.

process_types.energy_profile: Next-generation sequencing (whole exome, whole genome) enables rapid identification of causative mutations in PIDs, informing prognosis, treatment (HSCT, gene therapy), and family counseling.
process_types.direction: Approaches: Targeted gene panels (for specific PID categories) → whole exome sequencing (WES) → whole genome sequencing (WGS). Functional validation: flow cytometry (protein expression), immunoblotting, in vitro functional assays (e.g., DHR for CGD).
process_types.function: Definitive diagnosis; guide HSCT (conditioning regimen, donor selection); eligibility for gene therapy; prenatal diagnosis; family screening.
goals: Apply genetic diagnosis to clinical management

primary_example.explanation:
X-linked SCID (IL2RG deficiency) is the first PID successfully treated with gene therapy: (1) Principle: Autologous hematopoietic stem cells (HSCs) are harvested from patient; ex vivo transduction with lentiviral vector carrying functional IL2RG cDNA; transduced cells infused after conditioning. (2) Outcome: Reconstitution of T cell, B cell, NK cell function; immune recovery; clearance of infections; normal growth and development. (3) Comparison to HSCT: No need for donor (avoids GVHD); no immunosuppression; risk of insertional mutagenesis (early trials using γ-retroviral vectors had leukemia risk; newer lentiviral vectors have improved safety profile). (4) Indications: X-SCID, ADA-SCID, X-linked CGD, Wiskott-Aldrich syndrome. (5) Availability: Gene therapy is approved in Europe and US for some PIDs; remains limited to specialized centers.

Significance: Gene therapy exemplifies precision medicine—correcting the underlying genetic defect to restore immune function. Advances in vector design (self-inactivating lentiviral vectors, targeted integration) are improving safety and expanding applications.

image.alt: Gene therapy for X-SCID: harvest HSCs, transduce with lentiviral vector carrying IL2RG, condition, infuse transduced cells. Reconstituted T, B, NK cells. Safety: lentiviral vectors have improved integration profile.
image.caption: Gene Therapy for X-SCID: Autologous HSCs are transduced with functional IL2RG gene, restoring T, B, and NK cell development. Gene therapy eliminates GVHD risk and enables immune reconstitution without donor.

image.alt: Genetic diagnosis of PIDs: next-generation sequencing (WES/WGS) identifies mutations in genes such as IL2RG, ADA, RAG1, BTK, etc. Functional confirmation by flow cytometry (protein expression) or DHR (CGD).
image.caption: Genetic Diagnosis of Primary Immunodeficiencies: Next-generation sequencing identifies causative mutations, enabling definitive diagnosis, treatment selection (HSCT, gene therapy), and family counseling.

process_types.energy_profile: Genetic variants predict response and toxicity to immunomodulatory drugs, enabling personalized treatment selection.
process_types.direction: Examples: Thiopurine methyltransferase (TPMT) genotyping—predicts risk of myelosuppression from azathioprine, 6-mercaptopurine; HLA-B*5701 screening—prevents abacavir hypersensitivity; HLA-B*15:02—predicts carbamazepine-induced Stevens-Johnson syndrome in Asian populations; NUDT15 variants—predict thiopurine toxicity.
process_types.function: Prevent severe adverse drug reactions; optimize drug selection and dosing; improve treatment outcomes.
goals: Apply genetic testing to prevent adverse reactions

primary_example.explanation:
Azathioprine and 6-mercaptopurine are used in autoimmune diseases (IBD, lupus, vasculitis) and transplant immunosuppression. (1) Metabolism: Thiopurines are metabolized by TPMT (thiopurine methyltransferase) and other enzymes; TPMT activity is genetically determined. (2) Variants: TPMT*2, *3A, *3C are common loss-of-function alleles. (3) Phenotypes: Normal metabolizers (two normal alleles) → standard dosing; intermediate metabolizers (one normal, one variant) → reduced starting dose (50-70%); poor metabolizers (two variants) → extreme risk of myelosuppression; alternative therapy recommended. (4) Clinical impact: Pre-treatment genotyping (or phenotyping via RBC TPMT activity) reduces risk of severe neutropenia, pancytopenia. (5) Implementation: TPMT testing is standard of care before initiating azathioprine in many settings.

Significance: Pharmacogenomics exemplifies precision medicine in immune disorders—identifying patients at risk for severe adverse reactions before drug exposure, enabling safer prescribing.

image.alt: TPMT genotyping: normal metabolizers (standard dose), intermediate metabolizers (reduced dose), poor metabolizers (avoid azathioprine). Prevents thiopurine-induced myelosuppression.
image.caption: TPMT Genotyping: Pharmacogenomic testing identifies patients at risk for thiopurine-induced myelosuppression, enabling dose adjustment or alternative therapy selection.

image.alt: Pharmacogenomics in immune disorders: TPMT (azathioprine), HLA-B*5701 (abacavir), HLA-B*15:02 (carbamazepine), NUDT15 (thiopurines).
image.caption: Pharmacogenomics: Genetic variants predict drug toxicity and response, enabling personalized treatment selection and prevention of severe adverse reactions.

sections.relationship: Immune disorders encompass a spectrum from excessive immune activity (hypersensitivity, autoimmunity) to deficient activity (immunodeficiency). Understanding the underlying mechanisms—from hypersensitivity classification to autoimmune pathogenesis to genetic defects in immunodeficiency—enables accurate diagnosis, targeted therapy, and, in some cases, curative approaches (HSCT, gene therapy). Advances in immunogenetics and precision medicine are transforming the management of immune disorders, with personalized treatments based on molecular diagnosis and pharmacogenomic predictors.

summary:
Immune disorders result from dysregulation of the immune system—excessive or misdirected activity (hypersensitivity, autoimmunity) or deficient activity (immunodeficiency).

Hypersensitivity (Gell and Coombs classification): Type I (IgE-mediated, mast cell degranulation)—anaphylaxis, allergic rhinitis, asthma; treatment: antihistamines, epinephrine, anti-IgE. Type II (antibody-mediated cytotoxic)—autoimmune hemolytic anemia, Goodpasture syndrome, Graves' disease; treatment: immunosuppression, plasmapheresis, rituximab. Type III (immune complex-mediated)—SLE, serum sickness; treatment: immunosuppression, hydroxychloroquine. Type IV (T cell-mediated)—contact dermatitis, tuberculin reaction, multiple sclerosis; treatment: corticosteroids, calcineurin inhibitors, anti-TNF.

Autoimmune diseases arise from breakdown of self-tolerance: central tolerance failure (AIRE deficiency), peripheral tolerance failure (Treg dysfunction), molecular mimicry (rheumatic fever, Guillain-Barré), epitope spreading, and genetic susceptibility (HLA associations). Organ-specific: Hashimoto's, type 1 diabetes, multiple sclerosis, myasthenia gravis. Systemic: SLE, rheumatoid arthritis, systemic sclerosis, Sjögren's syndrome. Treatment: immunosuppression (corticosteroids, DMARDs), biologics (anti-TNF, anti-IL-6, anti-CD20, anti-BAFF), supportive care (hormone replacement, IVIG).

Immunodeficiencies: Primary (genetic)—B cell deficiencies (XLA, CVID, IgA deficiency) → recurrent encapsulated bacterial infections; T cell deficiencies (DiGeorge, SCID) → opportunistic infections; combined deficiencies (SCID) → severe early infections; phagocyte deficiencies (CGD) → catalase-positive bacteria, Aspergillus; complement deficiencies → encapsulated bacteria (C3), Neisseria (C5-C9). Secondary—HIV/AIDS (CD4+ T cell depletion), chemotherapy, splenectomy, malnutrition. Diagnosis: quantitative immunoglobulins, flow cytometry, vaccine titers, DHR, CH50, genetic testing. Treatment: antimicrobial prophylaxis, immunoglobulin replacement (IVIG), HSCT, gene therapy, ART for HIV.

Key principles: Infection pattern localizes immunodeficiency; hypersensitivity mechanism guides treatment; autoimmunity results from genetic susceptibility and environmental triggers; precision medicine (genetic diagnosis, pharmacogenomics, targeted biologics) is transforming management.

Understanding immune disorders—from mechanisms to clinical presentation to therapy—is foundational for allergy/immunology, rheumatology, infectious disease, and internal medicine.
